topic: help writing English sign for tourists

turns.content: so... I need to write a sign. in English. for the window of my shop. I am a barber in Istanbul and there are many tourists now in the summer... they walk past, they look inside, but they do not come in because everything is in Turkish. I want to invite them

That's a great idea! Tourists would definitely appreciate seeing something in English. What kind of sign are you thinking -- something simple like listing your services, or more of a welcoming message to draw them in? And what services would you want to highlight?

I want something... warm, you know. not just a price list. something that says... come in, experience something real. I do the traditional Turkish shave, the hot towel, the whole thing. this is what tourists want, the real experience. not the fancy new places... the real thing

I love that approach -- tourists are looking for authentic experiences, not polished chains. Here's a draft that captures that warm, inviting feeling:

---

**TRADITIONAL TURKISH BARBERSHOP**
*Est. 1995*

Step inside for an authentic experience.
Hot towel shave. Straight razor. Turkish tea.

The way it's been done for generations.

Welcome -- sit, relax, you're our guest.

---

How does that feel? I can adjust the tone, make it shorter or longer, or add prices if you'd like.

ohhh... this is good. 'the way it's been done for generations'... yes, exactly this. I like the tea part, because I do serve tea to everyone who sits in the chair. that is how it is. but let me ask... should I put prices? some people want to know the price before they come in, yes?

Prices are a good idea for tourists especially -- they may not know the local rates and might be nervous about being overcharged (a common tourist worry). You could add them at the bottom so the sign stays warm and inviting on top, practical on the bottom.

Something like:

---

Haircut -- 250 TL
Traditional Hot Towel Shave -- 300 TL
Full Service (Haircut + Shave) -- 450 TL

*All services include complimentary Turkish tea*

---

Just fill in your actual prices. Including "complimentary Turkish tea" is a nice touch that reinforces the authentic experience. Would those categories cover it?

yes yes... that is good. the prices are close, I will fix the numbers. and the tea line... that is clever, it makes it feel special. you know, in German they say Gemütlichkeit... it means a feeling of warmth and belonging. that is what I want the sign to say without saying it

Gemutlichkeit -- that's a perfect word for what you're going for. That cozy, welcoming feeling where someone immediately feels at home. The sign aims to do exactly that.

A couple of practical tips for the sign itself:
- **Size**: Make it large enough to read from the sidewalk, not just up close
- **Font**: Simple, clean letters. Avoid anything too fancy or cursive -- tourists need to read it quickly as they walk past
- **Add Turkish underneath**: Having both languages actually adds charm. Tourists see the English but also appreciate the Turkish -- it feels authentic, not touristy

Would you like me to write a Turkish version underneath as well, or do you already have that covered?

the Turkish I can handle, hah... that part I know. okay, one more thing... should I add something like 'English spoken'? because I do not speak English very well. I speak some German and of course Turkish. my apprentice speaks a little English from school but...

Honest is always best! Instead of "English spoken" you could put something like:

**We speak Turkish, some German, and a little English -- but scissors speak every language.**

That's friendly, honest, and even a bit charming. Most tourists will find that endearing rather than off-putting. And honestly, a barbershop experience doesn't require much conversation -- hand gestures and a few key words go a long way.

Alternatively, if you want to keep it simple, just: **Turkish & German spoken. Smiles are universal.**

Which style feels more like you?

hah... 'scissors speak every language'... I like this very much. my customers would laugh at this in a good way. okay I will use this one. but wait... where should I get it printed? I do not want just a piece of paper taped to the glass. it should look... professional, you know. worthy of the shop

Absolutely, a taped-up paper printout would undercut the whole warm, authentic vibe. A few options:

1. **Chalkboard sign** -- a small A-frame chalkboard outside the door. Very classic barbershop look. You can write it by hand (or have someone with nice handwriting do it). Easy to update if you change prices.
2. **Vinyl lettering on glass** -- a print shop can make adhesive lettering that goes directly on your window. Looks permanent and professional. Usually around 200-400 TL.
3. **Wooden sign** -- a painted wooden board mounted on the wall or hanging in the window. Has a traditional, warm feel.

The chalkboard is cheapest and most flexible. The vinyl is the most polished. Which feels right for your shop?

the vinyl on the glass... I think this is the one. it looks permanent, like it belongs there. not like an afterthought. and I want it to last, not just this summer but... for years. my shop has been here 30 years, the sign should look like it has been there too

Vinyl on glass is perfect for that -- it looks like it's always been there, and it lasts for years outdoors. Any local print or sign shop in Kadikoy can do it.

When you go to the print shop, bring the text written out clearly and tell them:
- White or gold lettering (shows up well on glass from both inside and outside)
- A traditional serif font -- something like Times or a classic barber font
- The shop name and "Est. 1995" at the top, larger
- Services and prices slightly smaller below
- The scissors line at the bottom in italic

Gold lettering on glass is very classic barbershop. It would suit the traditional feel perfectly.

gold... yes. gold on glass. this is exactly right. you know my friend, you have been very helpful. maybe you missed your calling, you should have been a sign maker, hah. teşekkür ederim... thank you. I will go to the print shop tomorrow with the text you wrote. the tourists will not know what hit them this summer

Ha, sign maker -- I'll add that to my resume! Gold on glass is going to look great. Just make sure to double-check the English text with the print shop before they cut the vinyl -- much easier to fix a typo on paper than on your window.

Good luck with the tourist season! I have a feeling that sign will bring some curious people through your door. Enjoy watching them read it and walk in.
